title: Dandelion root decoction
subtitle: A digestive bitter before meals
excerpt: Dried dandelion root simmered for 15 minutes and drunk before meals. The classic western digestive bitter preparation, traditionally taken to stimulate bile flow and support fat digestion.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, dandelion root entry: the simmered preparation method, the digestive bitter and bile-stimulating tradition. the herbal reference on Taraxaci radix cum herba (2009), established use for bile flow stimulation and dyspeptic complaints.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dried dandelion root stores 12 months in a sealed tin, cool and dark. Prepare fresh each cup; the simmered preparation oxidises and loses potency within a few hours.

glossaryTerms.definition: A preparation made by simmering harder plant material (roots, bark, seeds) in water to extract constituents that would not dissolve in a simple infusion.

content.text: drunk 15-20 minutes before the main meal: this is the traditional western digestive bitter preparation. The root is cut or granulated and simmered in water long enough to pull out the bitter taraxacin, which stimulates bile secretion when tasted. The bitterness is the action; diluting it with sweetener defeats the purpose. Dried root, 5 g to 250 ml water, 15 minutes at a gentle simmer. The resulting liquid is dark amber and perceptibly bitter.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Gallstones. Dandelion root strongly stimulates bile flow. If you have diagnosed gallstones with any obstruction of the bile duct, do not use this preparation.

Asteraceae allergy. Dandelion is in the daisy family. Readers with a known ragweed, chrysanthemum, or daisy allergy should approach with caution.

Diuretics and lithium. Regular therapeutic use of dandelion preparations has a mild diuretic effect. Readers on diuretic medication or lithium should consult their prescriber.

items.prepNote: dried and cut root, not the powder; cut root gives a cleaner decoction
cold start; the water will reduce to about 250 ml during the simmer

content.text: Combine the dried dandelion root and cold water in a small saucepan. Bring to a gentle simmer, then reduce the heat to maintain a slow, steady simmer for 15 minutes. Do not boil hard; a roiling boil drives off some of the volatile active compounds. The liquid should reduce to about 250 ml and turn dark amber. If it reduces more than this, add a splash of hot water to bring it back up.

Strain through a sieve into a cup. Press the spent root with the back of a spoon to extract the last of the liquid. The herbal simmered preparation should smell earthy and bitter, not rancid or musty; fresh-dried root produces a clean herbal simmered preparation. Drink warm, about 15-20 minutes before a meal, without sweetening.

One cup (250 ml, containing 5 g dried root) before the main meal, once daily. Take for up to 4 weeks at a time; if digestive symptoms persist, see your doctor. Paediatric use: not for children under twelve without specialist herbalist advice.

Prepare fresh each time. The herbal simmered preparation oxidises quickly and is best drunk within an hour of making. Dried dandelion root stores 12 months in a sealed tin, cool, dark, and away from moisture.
When not to use this remedy
Bile duct obstruction from gallstones: do not use. Persistent indigestion with weight loss, difficulty swallowing, or blood in stools needs urgent medical assessment.
